blurb: Stiff-caned roses trained upright on a wall or trellis, many reblooming all summer.
spread: Needs tying to a support — not self-clinging
seasons: Repeat flushes late spring–fall (by cultivar); hips & bare canes in winter
wildlife: Single-flowered types feed bees; hips feed birds
commons: Climbing rose Easton Lodge Gardens walled garden Little Easton Essex England 01.jpg

care.hardiness: Hardiness varies by cultivar — choose repeat-blooming climbers rated to USDA zone 4 or 5 for Front Range reliability (e.g. 'New Dawn', 'Fourth of July', 'Don Juan'). CSU Extension notes that climbing hybrid teas are generally not recommended in most of Colorado due to winter cane damage; focus on hardy own-root or zone-4/5 climbers that bloom on both old and new wood. Expect some cane dieback in harsh winters; prune out the dead wood in spring.
care.sun: Full sun — at least 6 hours daily, ideally 8. A south or west-facing wall or fence suits repeat-blooming climbers well on the Front Range, though a south wall can amplify heat; east-facing exposures (morning sun) help keep canes cooler and reduce mildew pressure.
care.soil: Rich, fertile, well-drained soil with good organic matter. Dig the planting hole 18–24 in wide and 18 in deep; amend Colorado's clay-loam generously with compost. Plant the bud union 2–4 in below the soil surface in Colorado for winter protection. Space the plant 12 in away from its support structure to allow airflow. Slightly acidic to neutral pH is preferred — alkaline soils may cause iron chlorosis; treat with chelated iron if yellowing appears.
care.water: Moderate and regular — approximately 1–2 in per week in summer. Water deeply at the base (drip or soaker hose) rather than overhead; wet foliage promotes fungal disease. Deep watering encourages roots to grow far down, making the plant more drought-resilient. Colorado's dry air means black spot (the most damaging rose disease) is far less of an issue than in humid climates, but powdery mildew and aphids are still common.
care.spacing: Space plants 3–4 ft apart along a wall or fence. Allow at least 12 in between the plant base and the support to permit airflow and ease of tying. Mature climbers reach 6–15 ft tall and 3–6 ft wide; choose support (trellis, wire espalier, or pergola) accordingly and install it before planting.
care.bloom: Repeat-blooming climbers produce flushes of bloom from late May through October on the Front Range, with the most spectacular show in June–July. The repeat bloom cycle runs every 4–6 weeks if deadheaded. One-time-blooming climbing species (uncommon in Colorado gardens) peak in June only.
care.feeding: Apply a granular rose fertilizer (or balanced slow-release fertilizer) in early spring as new growth emerges, then again in early summer after the first flush fades. Stop feeding by early August — late feeding pushes soft growth that is vulnerable to early fall frosts. Top-dress with a 2–3 in layer of compost in spring.
care.maintenance: Training is the key task: climbing roses are not self-clinging and must be tied to their support. Fan the main canes horizontally or at a gentle diagonal across wires or trellis — horizontal caning maximizes flowering along the length of each cane. Tie loosely with soft twine to avoid girdling. Prune in late winter (late February–late April on the Front Range — wait until about 2 weeks before the last frost, roughly late April in Boulder): remove dead, damaged, or crossing canes first; then shorten side shoots (laterals) on the main framework back by two-thirds. Do not remove the main framework canes unless they are old and unproductive — the goal is to maintain and extend the scaffold, not start over. For repeat-blooming climbers, you may also do light pruning after the first flush. In fall, shorten very long whippy canes to prevent wind damage but do not do the main structural prune until late winter.
care.troubles: Aphids are the most common pest on the Front Range — clusters on new shoots in spring; knock off with water or use insecticidal soap. Black spot (the most damaging disease in humid climates) is rarely severe in Colorado's dry air; still, water at the base and space plants for airflow. Powdery mildew can appear in crowded spots or during dry warm spells; improve airflow and avoid overhead watering. Rose rosette (spread by eriophyid mites) is an increasing threat on the Front Range — look for witches'-broom growth, excessive thorniness, or distorted red foliage; infected plants must be removed and disposed of entirely, no cure exists.
care.companions: Underplant with low companions that shade the root zone: catmint, hardy geraniums, lavender, or salvia. Climbing roses paired with Group 3 clematis (planted to scramble through the rose canes) is a classic combination — the two are pruned on compatible schedules and complement each other's bloom.
care.planting: Plant climbing roses in spring (April–May) or early fall (late August–September) on the Front Range — both seasons allow the root system to establish before temperature extremes. Spring planting in Boulder is most common; wait until the soil is workable and frost is not forecast for a week, typically April. Fall planting (by late September) gives roots several weeks to settle before freeze-up. Avoid planting in the heat of July–August. For in-ground planting, set the bud union 2–4 in below soil level for Colorado winter protection and water in well. In containers, climbing roses can be started but are a challenging long-term choice: pot roots are far less cold-hardy than in-ground roots (a zone-5 climber in a pot may behave like zone 6–7), so overwinter pots in an unheated garage or insulated structure and never let the root ball freeze solid. Containers also dry out quickly in Boulder's wind and sun — check moisture frequently all season.
care.propagation: Hardwood cuttings are the most reliable home method for own-root climbing roses: in late fall or early winter, take 8–12 in pencil-thick cuttings of fully ripened cane, cut just below a bud at the base and at a slant above a bud at the top, dip the base in rooting hormone, and insert two-thirds of the cutting into a pot of gritty compost or into a sheltered nursery bed. Keep just moist and protect from hard freezes; roots form over winter and the cutting leafs out in spring. Semi-ripe cuttings in mid-to-late summer (6–8 in, with leaves retained on the top few nodes) also root well under a cloche or plastic tent. Grafting onto rootstock (the commercial standard, giving more vigorous plants) requires skill and specialized understock — not a practical home method. Own-root roses from cuttings are preferable for Front Range gardeners: if the top is killed by an extreme winter, any regrowth is the named cultivar rather than the rootstock.
